coredata.dc:title: Green fabrication of fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots from Aegle marmelos leaves for highly selective detection of Fe3+ metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Rishabh,
Rani, Manviri
Shanker, Uma
Singh Kaith, Balbir
Sillanpää, Mika
coredata.dc:description: This current study uses a simple, environmentally friendly, and economically advantageous Microwave method to create water-soluble carbon dots from Aegle marmelos leaves. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) that have been produced exhibit outstanding benefits such as water solubility, biocompatibility, and high stability. Essential characterization methods are used to examine it, including XRD, FTIR, HR-TEM, XPS, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. It exhibits excitation-dependent emission behavior, blue emission under UV lamp illumination (wavelength 365 nm), and pH independence. Synthesized N-CQDs with a quantum yield of 14.21 % responded exceptionally well to Fe3+ ions, which causes fluorescence to quench. A single probe fluorescence-based system for detecting Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity was designed. Under optimized fluorescence experimental conditions, the linear range of Fe3+ ions was 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 0.184 µM (R2 = 0.99363). The produced N-CQDs respond exceptionally well to Fe3+ ions, which causes fluorescence to quench. This study demonstrates a single probe FL-based device for detecting Fe3+ ions with high sensitivity and selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aegle Marmelos
N-CQDs
Fluorescent “turn-off” sensor
Selective detection
Fe3+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples

Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology Jalandhar

Dr Uma Shanker thanks DST, SERB, NEw Delhi (File Number: EEQ/2023/000196) and IIC, NIT Jalandhar, for instrumental support in characterization facilities. Mr Rishabh, thank the Ministry of Education (MoE), New Delhi, for the research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS spectra of N-CQDs. (a) Full XPS spectrum. (b-d). Deconvoluted C1s, O1s and N1s spectra.
(a) FTIR spectrum (b) XRD spectrum of N-CQDs (c) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of N-CQDs and N-CQDs/ Fe3+.

(a) Absorbance spectrum of N-CQDs (inset: blue emission under UV lamp. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots at different excitation wavelength ranging from 200 − 250 nm (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots at 240 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
N-CQDs stability studies (a) Effect of pH on PL intensity (b) number of days (c) different time periods (d) ionic strength of KCl.

(a) Emission spectra of N, S-CQDs at different temperature values. (b) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity change ratio F 0/F versus temperature (25–65 °C). (c) Temperature effect on the fluorescence emission of N, S-CQDs: cyclic test.

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the N-CQDs solutions containing different metal ions. (b) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentration of Fe3+ ions (1 to 250 μM) (c) Calibration graph for the determination of the Fe3+ ions (d) Absorption spectra for N-CQDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+ ions (e) Interference test of N-CQDs solution for Fe3+ ions and other metal ions. (f) Comparison experiment using fluorescent lamp irradiation.
Schematic representation of synthesis of N-CQDs.
Schematic representation of Fe3+ sensing by N-CQDs.
Comparison the LOD values of different carbon dot- based fluorescent turn-off probe for detection of Fe ions.

row.entry: Kiwi fruit peels + ammonium hydroxide (plant source)
Bombyx mori silk + Citric acid (animal source)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of Fe3 + in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Green fabrication of fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots from Aegle marmelos leaves for highly selective detection of Fe3+ metal ions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Chemistry, Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology, Jalandhar, Punjab 144008, India. (U. Shanker). Department of Chemistry, Malaviya National Institute of Technology, Jaipur, Rajasthan 302017, India. (M. Rani).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr B R Ambedkar National Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This current study uses a simple, environmentally friendly, and economically advantageous Microwave method to create water-soluble carbon dots from Aegle marmelos leaves. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CQDs) that have been produced exhibit outstanding benefits such as water solubility, biocompatibility, and high stability. Essential characterization methods are used to examine it, including XRD, FTIR, HR-TEM, XPS, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. It exhibits excitation-dependent emission behavior, blue emission under UV lamp illumination (wavelength 365 nm), and pH independence. Synthesized N-CQDs with a quantum yield of 14.21 % responded exceptionally well to Fe3+ ions, which causes fluorescence to quench. A single probe fluorescence-based system for detecting Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity was designed. Under optimized fluorescence experimental conditions, the linear range of Fe3+ ions was 0–20 μM with a detection limit of 0.184 µM (R2 = 0.99363). The produced N-CQDs respond exceptionally well to Fe3+ ions, which causes fluorescence to quench. This study demonstrates a single probe FL-based device for detecting Fe3+ ions with high sensitivity and selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ ions are essential for the functioning of all living organisms, playing a crucial role in metabolic processes such as the transport of molecular oxygen, synthesis of nucleic acids, and electron transport. During infections, living cells rely on the presence of these ions to sustain their function, metabolism, and proliferation [1]. However, an excessive accumulation of Fe3+ beyond physiological levels adversely affects various cellular structures and activities in organs such as the heart, pancreas, kidney, liver, and others [2]. Iron overload also impacts bone marrow function [3] and is linked to cancer development [4]. While the accumulation of Fe3+ in humans is primarily associated with contaminated food products [5], its origin can be traced back to the leaching of iron from the weathering of iron silicates and other minerals into the aquatic environment in the form of hydrated iron oxide [6]. Since ferric ions are toxic to fish and other marine organisms [7], it is crucial to develop rapid monitoring techniques for their presence, even at deficient concentrations found in natural waters. Various methods, including spectrophotometry, flow-injection analysis, graphite furnace atomic absorption, and high-resolution electrospray ionization mass spectrometry, exist for this purpose. Among them, the fluorescent method [8,9,10] is considered the most suitable due to its rapidity, high sensitivity, selectivity, and freedom from tedious pre-treatment processes. Although there are numerous “turn-on” fluorescent probes for Fe (III) [11], recent studies have explored the use of carbon quantum dots as “turn-off” sensors for various metal ions, including Fe3+ [12,13,14].

Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs), are a new class of carbon-based nanomaterial that has gained importance due to its outstanding physiochemical properties like low cost, stability, chemical inertness, abundant, biocompatibility, tunable photoluminescence (PL) property and easy surface modification [15–17], is the most widely studied multispecialty nanomaterial. Each CQD can trap charge carriers that glow at various wavelengths depending on the energy band structure [18]. CQDs stand out from conventional Q-dots (Like CdTe and ZnS Q-dots) due to their reduced toxicity, affordability, improved biocompatibility, and ease of production [19]. Long-term ultraviolet exposure has little effect on their photostability [20]. CQDs with intriguing features were initially created with diameters > 10 nm [21]. CQDs have been produced utilizing various techniques over time, including ultrasonication, electrochemical oxidation, microwave assistance, thermal approaches (such as solvothermal and hydrothermal), [22–26].

CQDs are viable candidates in a variety of domains, including sensor [27–30], bio-imaging [31], fluorescence ink [32], and catalysis [33]. These characteristics make CQDs a valuable replacement for traditional semiconductor Q-dots and polymer dots that have previously been reported. Over the years, several predecessors have been employed to create CQDs. The main obstacle is to increase the PL property of CQDs. This is done by doping heteroatoms like S, N, B, and P or with Ag and Au on the surface of the CQDs, which results in more defects and binding sites with increased PL properties. Due to its ease of interacting with carbon atoms, N-doped CQDs are among those that are investigated the most to change the PL property [31,32 34–36].

Nitrogen is a typical charge carrier atom [37], when synthesizing CQDs its radius is comparable to carbon's. Because its outermost shell contains five valence electrons, it may bond carbon atoms strongly [38]. Nitrogen is also called the n-type donor atom because of its charge carrier property [39]. CQDs can quench their self-fluorescence by transferring their electrons at the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the substrate at the time of excitation. N-type nitrogen is abundant on their surfaces, which may be helpful for [40] CQDs to increase the range of their analytical uses and detection applications. Many chemical-free green synthesis methods using materials or sources for CQDs readily available in nature have already been documented [41]. For instance, fennel seeds and lemon juice are used to make CQDs [42], but there are few for biomass waste [43,44]. Phyllanthus acidus was employed as a precursor in the [45] Atchudan group's synthetic process for nitrogen-doped CQDs, which can be used to detect Fe3+ ions precisely. To detect Fe3+ ions, [46] used CQDs made from pine wood as its raw material. As a probe for sensing Fe3+ ions, [47] created N-CQDs using a hydrothermal method with gelatin as a precursor. G-CDs have been designed for on-site selective detection of Fe3+ ions [48]. Phosphorus and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for iron(III) [49]. Green phosphorous and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (C-dots) for detecting copper ions [50]. P-CNDs synthesized for sensing of Zn(II) and Cd(II) ions [51,52] .

In this study, we first investigated the utilization of Aegle Marmelos leaves as a novel precursor for synthesizing photoluminous carbon dots. This approach eliminates the requirement for costly and hazardous chemicals and labor-intensive synthetic procedures. Aegle Marmelos leaves a member of the Rutaceae family and is readily available on campus throughout the year. Aegle Marmelos, are renowned for their potential in treating various health conditions such as diabetes, cholesterol issues, peptic ulcers, inflammation, and diarrhea [53]. Aegle Marmelos exhibits the presence of phytochemicals, including γ-sitosterol, rutin, β-sitosterol, glycosides, marmeline, aegelin, marmesinin, halfordiol, phenyl ethyl cinnamamides and lupeol [54]. While there are existing studies on synthesizing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from plant extracts, our synthesis method includes green plant percussor with water rather than harsh chemicals and toxic solvents. Synthesized N-CQDs are easy to prepare, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and potential for large-scale production. The resulting nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) exhibit notable stability and water solubility, and emit blue UV light fluorescence. Particularly in Fe3+ detection, these synthesized N-CQDs demonstrate high selectivity and sensitivity with a low detection limit for Fe3+, the N-CQDs function as a singular probe device for sensing Fe3+ in aqueous solutions.

Leaves of Aegle Marmelos (Bael Patra) were collected from the plant in our Institute (NIT Jalandhar, Punjab, India). All nitrates of Pb2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and Zn2+ purchased from LOBA and deionized water was used in all experiments. All reagents were of AR grade.

5 g leaves of Aegle Marmelos were picked and washed with double distilled water to remove dirt and other impurities. The leaves were crushed with a household mixer. For nitrogen doping, the suspension solution was prepared by taking 2 g of solid urea 10 mL deionized water and added to the paste of leaves. Then, the resultant extract was diluted with 40 mL of distilled water and irradiated to microwave waves (1200 W for 5 min). Afterwards, 50 mL DI water in the above solution was filtered to separate solid particles (Scheme 1 ). The clear solution was put in a dialysis membrane(1000 Da) for 48 h to remove large particles and stored in a freezer at 4 ◦C for further use.

For the selectivity study of various metal ions (Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Cr3+ and Cr6+) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, 1 mL of 500 μM metal ion solution was mixed with 25 μL of N-CQDs solution fluorescence behavior were recorded after stabilizing for 10 min. The same method was carried out using N-CQDs- Fe3+ mixture, resulting in final Fe3+ concentrations ranging from 1 to 4 μM.

The average size, surface morphology, and elemental composition of the N-CQD particles were investigated using TEM and FE-SEM analysis. The FE-SEM images revealed the spherical morphology [Figure S1], and the EDX analysis [Figure S2]. N-CQDs exhibit a composition of C = 42.29 %, N = 17.91 %, and O = 39.80 %, while plain CQDs demonstrate a composition of C = 48.9 %, N = 3.63 %, and O = 47.45 %, as illustrated in Figure S2. The higher percentage of nitrogen in the sample shows the doping of CQDs; more characterizations have been done to check the successful doping of nitrogen onto CQDs [55]. HR-TEM images revealed that the N-CQDs had a diameter that fell within a tight size distribution range of 4–10 nm. The average diameter was estimated as 6.64 nm [Fig. 1 b]. The SAED pattern's diffracted rings indicated an amorphous polycrystalline structure consistent with the XRD findings [56]. The acquired TEM data confirm the designed N-CQDs monodispersity.

The elemental composition of N-CQDs was investigated using XPS measurement, and the surface functional groups were verified. Fig. 2 shows the XPS survey of N-CQDs which has three peaks that correspond to the binding energies of C 1S, N 1S, and O 1S at 284.4 eV, 399.4 eV, and 532.2 eV, respectively. Further deconvolution of the C1s revealed three distinct chemical environments at binding energies of 284.7 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.3 eV, which are ascribed to the C = C/C–C, C = O and C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The single peak at 531.1 eV in the O 1S high-resolution spectrum seen in Fig. 2c is attributable to the C—O— bond, respectively [57]. Two peaks in the N 1S spectrum, at 398.1 eV and 400.1 eV, show the presence of C-N-C and N-C3 bonds, respectively (Fig. 2b and d). The existence of active surface functional sites and the effective doping of N heteroatoms into the CQDs are demonstrated by all these results, which support the FTIR data [58,59].

The FTIR spectrum [Fig. 3 a] verified the existence of functional groups in N-CQDs. The distinctive band breaks between 3300 and 3600 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of OH and NH. It is believed that C = O and C-O-NH and stretching vibration caused the peak at 1630 cm−1. The feature peaks centered at 1550 and 1485 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of C = N and C-N, respectively [60,61]. These findings suggest that N-CQDs become more soluble in aqueous solution due to the active functional sites on their surfaces. All these stretching and vibration bands show that the surface of N-CQDs has multiple functional groups, including hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide, along with nitrogen and oxygen-rich molecules without any impurities, consistent with the XPS results [62]. In addition, the absolute fluorescence QY of the prepared N-CQDs was measured to be 14.21 % (Figure S3) (the detailed calculation of the absolute QY can be found in the Supporting Information).

X-ray diffraction measurements were used to investigate the structural analysis and purity of N-CQDs. The diffraction pattern revealed a prominent peak at 2θ = 23°, which matches the (002) plane Fig. 3b. Using Bragg's law, Carbon dots have an interlayer spacing value of 3.85 A°. Greater than bulk graphite (3.4 A°), which clarifies why carbon dots are amorphous [63]. This amorphous form may be due to the tightly packed C atoms and many oxygen-containing active functional groups on N-CQDs' surface [61]. These findings claim that the CQDs made from A. Marmelos leaves are pure since they only show the distinctive peaks of carbon dots.

The UV–visible spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 4 a) shows a hump at 287 and 325 nm, which refers to electron transition of ᴨ → ᴨ* (C = C) and n → ᴨ* (C = O & C-O), respectively. In daylight, the color of N-CQDs seemed light brown and appeared bright blue under UV light (λ = 365 nm) Fig. 4 b. (Fig shows synthesized N-CQDs excitation dependent emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 200 to 250 nm. The maximum intensity was recorded at 240 nm excitation wavelength. With the increase and decrease in excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength red-shifted with a concomitant reduction in intensity. This might be due to the existence of various particle sizes as well as surface functional sites [64]. When exposed to UV radiation, the CQDs and N-CQDs exhibit distinct dull and bright blue emissions, respectively. A comparison of the excitation-dependent emission spectra for both CQDs and N-CQDs reveals variations in intensities under a 240 nm excitation wavelength, as illustrated in Figure S4. Despite numerous reported efforts, there still needs to be discussion over the origin of N-CQD fluorescence. The surface state and confinement effect may have combined to cause the fluorescence. Surface defects and various particle sizes created the excitation-dependent emission spectra of N-CQDs. On the other hand, nitrogen-bonded functional groups produced by doping formed by surface oxidation significantly impact the band gap and result in a red-shifted fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDS [65].

To further clarify the pH-dependent characteristic of N-CQDs, 25 μL of N-CQD solution was diluted in 5 mL of ultrapure water and vortexed for 60 s. The change in the fluorescence emission intensity was investigated under different pH values (3–10) on pH meter. The obtained data are displayed in Fig. 5 a using a 1 M HCL and NaOH, the calibration initiated with buffer solution of pH 7 and pH 3, to basic (pH 8–10) and acidic (pH 3–6) as necessary of N-CQDs solutions. The N-CQDs solution's pH value was determined to be 7; PL intensity was seen to be the highest at this level. The ideal pH for the duration of the experiment has been assumed to be 7. We found that the PL intensity slightly decreased with the pH change from acidic to fundamental of the N-CQDs solution. The significant changes by OH– or H + ions over the functional groups may cause these variations in PL intensity. This characteristic could be explained by the deprotonation and protonation behavior of the functional groups found on the N-CQDs surfaces [66]. The emission intensity of the N-CQD solution at λ = 240 nm was recorded after 30 days to observe the stability of N-CQDs [Fig. 5b]. This solution was kept in the fridge at 5 °C between measurements. A slight decrease in fluorescence intensity, which is negligible, was seen on the 30th day when Fig. 5b was studied in greater detail. The findings of testing the photostability of N-CQDs using a UV lamp for 30 min and 365 nm radiation are shown in Fig. 5c. The robust photostability of N-CQDs is confirmed because irradiation differs from non-irradiation only a little in fluorescence intensity [67]. As the solution became more acidic or alkaline, the PL intensities decreased. As shown in Fig. 5d, there was little difference in the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs when the KCl solution's ionic strength was changed from 0 to 2 M. All of the findings mentioned above provide evidence in favor of the PL stability of the N-CQDs.

A 25 μL solution of N-CQDs was diluted to 5 mL using ultrapure water and subjected to incubation in a constant temperature water bath oscillator (200 rpm/min) ranging from 25 to 65 °C for 30 min each. The results, presented in Fig. 6 a, indicate a decline in N-CQD fluorescence intensity as the temperature increases. A linear correlation was observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) and temperature within the 25–65 °C range (Fig. 6b). The resulting linear equation is y = 0.01079x (R2 = 0.98266), where y signifies the fluorescence intensity ratio, and x denotes the temperature.

To validate the reversibility of N-CQD fluorescence intensity, the N-CQDs were heated to 25–65 °C and then returned to room temperature to observe the change in fluorescence intensity. Fig. 6c illustrates that when the temperature was reverted to 25 °C, the fluorescence intensity was restored. This result highlights the remarkable reversibility of N-CQDs, demonstrating that the temperature in the 25–65 °C range does not cause permanent damage to the surface fluorescence structure of N-CQDs. In summary, the synthesized N-CQD fluorescent nanoprobes exhibit promising potential for developing a temperature sensor.
Considering the temperature- and pH-sensitive properties of the synthesized N-CQDs, all subsequent experiments were conducted at room temperature and under neutral pH conditions (pH = 7).

For the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions and other metal ions, newly prepared N-CQDs were equilibrated with metal ions solution (Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Na+, Al3+). The fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs were studied with the addition of 500 μM metal ion solution (Fe3+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Na+, Al3+)at 240 nm excitation wavelength of to look at the selectivity characteristics of the N-CQDs. Different quenching levels and variations in the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs for various metal ions were observed from the fluorescence spectra, as shown in Fig. 7 a. The spectrum also reveals that the fluorescence emission intensity of N-CQDs with Fe3+ ions is lower than that of other metal ions, suggesting specific interactions between Fe3+ ions and CQDs [Fig. 7b]. Absorption spectra for N-CQDs of Fe3+ ions in the presence and absence and interference test of N-CQDs solution for Fe3+ ions and other metal ions were also recorded [Fig. 7 d and e]. Further comparison experiment of N-CQD solution with metal ion solutions was done under UV light (λ = 365 nm) [Fig. 7f]. The quenching mechanism of fluorescence is investigated using the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv [X] plot, as illustrated in Fig. 7c. In this case, F and F0 are the fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs solution with Fe3+ ions and blank, respectively. At the same time, X is the concentration of Fe3+ ions (ranging 1 to 4 μM) dissolved in the N-CQDs solution, and Ksv is the linear fit slope. This plot led us to conclude that there was a linear relationship between F0/F and the conc. of Fe3+ ions. As a result, an observation is made that there is an R2 correlation coefficient between the conc. of Fe3+ ions and the quenching efficiency. The LOD was calculated by the equation:
(1)
LOD = 3 σ / m
where σ is the standard deviation of the N-CQDs blank signal and m is the slope of the linear fit. The conclusion is that the as-synthesized N-CQDs can be broadly used in the methodology's sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions. The LOD values measured for Fe3+ ions were 0.184 µM, which is noticeably lower than the recommended threshold (300 µM) set by the World Health Organisation (WHO). Additionally, we contrasted our method's linear range and detection limit with those of previously published N-CQDs grounded Fe3+ sensors in Table 1 . It is evident from the results in Table 1 that this method is advantageous over the other techniques, including a wide linear range and lower LOD. To explore the selectivity of N-CQDs, the tolerance limit experiments were performed to demonstrate the maximum concentration of interfering metal ions that did not cause a change in the signal detection of Fe3+ more than 5 %. The results presented in Table S1 showed that the tolerance limit for Zn2+ and Cu2+ was relatively low.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in actual water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The typical addition approach was applied to actual water samples drawn from our laboratory's tap water to take advantage of the real-world applicability of N-CQDs sensors. Tap water was boiled to remove chlorine and filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter to reduce experiment error. No iron was found in the tap water, so a recovery experiment was performed on the water sample after it was spiked with a standard amount of Fe3+ (5, 10, 15, 20 μM). Table 2 displays the equivalent recovery (%) for these samples, spanning 97.85 % to 105.14 %. The experiment was repeated three times to calculate the relative standard deviation. The results show excellent results with previously published investigations [68], and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for all data is > 1 %. This supports the suggested method's excellent efficiency and dependability.

Based on the characterizations mentioned above, the produced N-CQDs were proven to have a negative (-ve) surface charge and an abundance of functional groups like hydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups on their surfaces. Strong interaction between Fe3+ ions and N-CQDs results in the development of metal complexes because of the (-ve) charged N-CQDs and lively functional groups on their surfaces. The emission intensity decreases when Fe3+ ions come into contact with the N-CQDs.

In summary, this research successfully synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) using urea as a green nitrogen dopant, and Aegle Marmelos leaves as a starting material, eliminating the need for toxic chemicals. The selected method promotes environmental sustainability and proves economically advantageous, making it well-suited for large-scale production. Analysis through XRD and HR-TEM revealed that the N-CQDs were amorphous, spherical, and approximately 6.64 nm in size. Further investigation using XPS, FTIR, and EDS confirmed the successful nitrogen doping on the surface of the N-CQDs. Under UV radiation, the synthesized N-CQDs exhibited a blue emission with a remarkable 14.21 % quantum yield (QY). The N-CQDs demonstrated high selectivity and detection capabilities for Fe3+ through fluorescence quenching. With a limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ of 0.184 µM, this sensing technology was compared favorably with other carbon quantum dots in the provided table. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was attributed to the transfer of electrons from the excited fluorophores on N-CQDs' surface to the vacant d orbitals of iron. Practical applicability was tested by conducting experiments on actual water samples, resulting in a recovery rate of Fe3+ ions ranging from 97.85 % to 105.14 %. This eco-friendly, rapid, and cost-effective sensing technology holds great promise for detecting Fe3+ ions in environmental samples. In conclusion, the synthesized N-CQDs present a viable solution for efficient and sustainable sensing of Fe3+ ions, with potential applications in environmental monitoring.

Rishabh: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis. Manviri Rani: Conceptualization, Data curation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Uma Shanker: . Balbir Singh Kaith: Supervision, Validation, Visualization. Mika Sillanpää: Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr Manviri Rani thanks MRC, MNIT Jaipur, India for the financial assistance. Dr Uma Shanker thanks DST, SERB, NEw Delhi (File Number: EEQ/2023/000196) and IIC, NIT Jalandhar, for instrumental support in characterization facilities. Mr Rishabh, thank the Ministry of Education (MoE), New Delhi, for the research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111878.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Iron in infection and immunity
Mol. Aspects Med.

Clin. Chim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sousa, L., Oliveira, M. M., Pessoa, M. T. C., & Barbosa, L. A. (2020). Iron overload: Effects on cellular biochemistry. Clinica Chimica Acta, 504, 180-189., https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cca.2019.11.029.
